coredata.dc:title: A novel effective carbon dots-based inhibitor for carbon steel against chloride corrosion: From inhibition behavior to mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Li, Xue-Qi
Zheng, Shu-Yi
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Reinforced concrete (RC) structures suffer from serious chloride corrosion. Corrosion inhibitors can effectively protect reinforcement against chloride corrosion, thus improving the durability of RC structures. Benefiting from their environmental friendliness, simple preparation, and high inhibition efficiency, carbon dots (CDs) as a promising green corrosion inhibitor have attracted intensive attention. However, there is no investigation on the corrosion inhibition performance and mechanism of CDs in a chloride-contaminated alkaline medium for carbon steel, thereby hindering their application in RC structures. In the current research, CDs as a novel effective corrosion inhibitor were first applied to protect carbon steel in a chloride-contaminated simulated concrete pore solution (Cl-SCPS). Specifically, their inhibition behavior was investigated using electrochemical impedance spectra and potentiodynamic polarization measurements; And the inhibition mechanism was revealed by adsorption isotherm analysis, corrosion morphology study, and theoretical calculation. Results showed that CDs could achieve long-term inhibition efficiency of 99.2 % only at 400 mg/L on carbon steel in Cl-SCPS. Notably, the inhibition mechanism of CDs was ascribed to their stable adsorption on Fe (110) or γ-FeOOH (010) surfaces of carbon steel, with significantly higher E binding absolute values compared to previous literature. This research provided a novel high-performance inhibitor, as well as the facile synthesis method, to effectively mitigate chloride corrosion of carbon steel, expected to improve the durability of RC structures.

head.ce:title: A novel effective carbon dots-based inhibitor for carbon steel against chloride corrosion: From inhibition behavior to mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reinforced concrete (RC) structures suffer from serious chloride corrosion. Corrosion inhibitors can effectively protect reinforcement against chloride corrosion, thus improving the durability of RC structures. Benefiting from their environmental friendliness, simple preparation, and high inhibition efficiency, carbon dots (CDs) as a promising green corrosion inhibitor have attracted intensive attention. However, there is no investigation on the corrosion inhibition performance and mechanism of CDs in a chloride-contaminated alkaline medium for carbon steel, thereby hindering their application in RC structures. In the current research, CDs as a novel effective corrosion inhibitor were first applied to protect carbon steel in a chloride-contaminated simulated concrete pore solution (Cl-SCPS). Specifically, their inhibition behavior was investigated using electrochemical impedance spectra and potentiodynamic polarization measurements; And the inhibition mechanism was revealed by adsorption isotherm analysis, corrosion morphology study, and theoretical calculation. Results showed that CDs could achieve long-term inhibition efficiency of 99.2 % only at 400 mg/L on carbon steel in Cl-SCPS. Notably, the inhibition mechanism of CDs was ascribed to their stable adsorption on Fe (110) or γ-FeOOH (010) surfaces of carbon steel, with significantly higher E binding absolute values compared to previous literature. This research provided a novel high-performance inhibitor, as well as the facile synthesis method, to effectively mitigate chloride corrosion of carbon steel, expected to improve the durability of RC structures.

ce:section.ce:para: Reinforced concrete (RC) structures play an indispensable part in civil engineering due to their inexpensive raw materials, convenient production, and excellent mechanical properties [1–4]. Unfortunately, in highly corrosive environments, even high-quality concrete fails to maintain the passivation of its embedded reinforcement over an extended period. This threatens human safety and leads to structural deterioration, resulting in significant financial losses. For instance, the actual service life of constructed RC structures is far less than their intended service life; And only in 2014, the total corrosion cost for the roads and bridges in China was 62.37 billion CNY [5]. The main cause of RC structure deterioration is reinforcement corrosion arising from chloride contamination [6]. When chloride ingresses to steel surface through concrete cover and then accumulates to a threshold concentration, the steel will begin to corrode, causing the expansion of steel and then the crack of RC structures. As a result, multitudes of methods have been developed to prevent chloride-induced reinforcement corrosion, including electrochemical protection [7], anti-corrosion coatings [8], chloride binding [9], and corrosion inhibitors [10]. Among them, corrosion inhibitors are a widely accepted technique benefiting from their low cost, convenience, and high protective capability.

Up to now, corrosion inhibitors fall into two categories based on their chemical composition: inorganic inhibitors and organic inhibitors. Inorganic inhibitors, such as nitrates, chromates, and phosphates have shown promising inhibiting performance [11]. However, due to their hazardous effects, they do not meet environmental protection requirements. To address this issue, several organic inhibitors have emerged, including amines and alkanol amines [12], amino acids [13], silanes [14], ionic liquids [15], and plant extracts [16–18]. Among them, plant extracts and amino acids have been deemed as green corrosion inhibitors, attracting growing interest due to their environmentally friendly nature. However, these alternatives still have some drawbacks. In detail, preparing plant extracts often requires toxic solvents and involves tedious procedures, leading to environmental issues. In addition, amino acids are not cost-effective, limiting their applications. As a result, a crucial need is to develop an eco-friendly and straightforward method to produce a novel and effective green corrosion inhibitor.

Exhilaratingly, carbon dots (CDs) are cost-effective, ecologically acceptable, and easy to prepare, thereby serving as a viable solution for the next generation of green corrosion inhibitors [19,20]. Typically, CDs are defined as zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles with the size less than 10 nm and fluorescence acting as their inherent property [21]. Up to now, CDs have been extensively applied in biomedicine, sensing, catalysis, lubrication, and other fields [22–30]. However, their application in the field of metal corrosion protection starts late. In 2017, CDs were first demonstrated to be an efficient corrosion inhibitor [31]. More specifically, CDs were synthesized via solvothermal method and served as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in an HCl solution with an inhibition efficiency of over 90 % at 100 mg/L. Subsequent investigations also demonstrated the corrosion inhibition capabilities of CDs on copper, aluminum, and mild steel in the acidic environment, further bolstering the promising potential of CDs as highly effective corrosion inhibitors [32–37]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no investigation into the corrosion prevention capabilities and mechanism of CDs in alkaline settings, particularly chloride-contaminated alkaline environments. Furthermore, almost all studies employ a one-pot hydrothermal method to prepare CDs, addressing the laborious preparation process. Nevertheless, the heating process requires high energy and a large amount of time [38], leading to the rising cost inevitably, thus undermining the benefits of CDs-based corrosion inhibitors [39]. Therefore, it is highly urgent to develop a facile synthesis method for CDs and reveal their corrosion inhibition performance and mechanism on carbon steel in a chloride-contaminated alkaline corrosion medium.

In this work, CDs, prepared under a facile low-temperature method, first acted as a novel green corrosion inhibitor to protect carbon steel against chloride corrosion in the alkaline environment (namely, simulated concrete pore solution (SCPS)). Specifically, the facile low-temperature method was applied to prepare CDs at only 50 °C for 2 h, and their corrosion inhibition ability for carbon steel in chloride-contaminated SCPS (Cl-SCPS) was investigated through electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements. More importantly, the inhibition mechanism of CDs was unveiled by adsorption isotherm analysis, corrosion morphology investigation, and theoretical calculation. These findings provided a novel method to prepare an efficient inhibitor for carbon steel in Cl-SCPS, with promising applications for enhancing the durability of RC structures.

The o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were obtained through Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) purchased from Aladdin Reagent Shanghai Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) was used for the preparation of SCPS. Sodium chloride (NaCl) purchased from Aladdin Reagent was employed for the preparation of Cl-SCPS. Q235 carbon steel (including 0.20 % C, 0.30 % Si, 1.20 % Mn, 0.25 % Cr, 0.045 % P, 0.05 % S, 0.25 % Cu, 0.25 % Ni and bal. Fe) was adopted as the test electrode. Before tests, the Q235 carbon steel samples underwent a sequential polishing process using SiC abrasive paper ranging from 400 to 2000 grit, followed by washing with DI water, degreasing with ethanol, and eventually drying with cold airflow.

Inspired by previous literature, the preparation of CDs was intentionally designed in the following manner [40,41]. Initially, 0.8 g p-BQ and 0.8 g o-PD were added to 160 mL DI water. And then after ultrasonic treatment, a uniformly mixed solution was subsequently shaken for 2 h at 50 °C by a shaking bath. The solution was then purified through a combination of centrifugation, vacuum filtration, and dialysis to remove residual impurities. In the end, rotary evaporation and vacuum freeze-drying methods were employed to yield solid-state brown CDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200X G2), and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon) were employed to observe the morphology of CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, PerkinElmer Lambda 750) spectra from 500 to 3500 cm−1, and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) with an Al Kα excitation source were taken to determine the chemical groups and compositions of CDs. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis, PerkinElmer Lambda 750) adsorption spectra, and Photoluminescence (PL, Hitachi F-7000) spectra were carried out to confirm the long-term dispersion stability of CDs in SCPS.

Electrochemical measurements were performed using a PARSTAT 4000+ electrochemical station at room temperature. The test solution was prepared as follows: saturated Ca(OH)2 solution functioned as the SCPS. 3.5 wt% NaCl was added into SCPS to form Cl-SCPS solutions. CDs were directly added to Cl-SCPS under stirring with a glass rod for several minutes to form 100, 200, 300, and 400 mg/L CDs solutions, named as C100, C200, C300, and C400. A classical three-electrode system was adopted to record the EIS and PDP tests, with a Hg/HgO reference electrode (RE), a platinum plate as the counter electrode (CE) and a working electrode (WE) made of pre-passivated Q235 carbon steel with an exposure area of 1 cm2. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , prior to the exposure to Cl-SCPS, WE specimens were immersed in SCPS for 7 d to simulate pre-passivation in reinforced concrete (RC) structures. The open circuit potential (OCP) was expected to reach a stable state before test. EIS was recorded from 105 to 10−2 Hz with 10 mV amplitude, and PDP was executed in a potential range from −250 mV to +250 mV versus OCP at 1 mV s−1 scanning rate. ZSimDemo 3.30 software was used to analyze and fit the EIS data.

Following the electrochemical measurements, the steel specimen underwent washing, drying, and subsequent storage in a vacuum oven to facilitate corrosion morphology observation. The corrosion morphology of the steel specimen was observed using Keyence VR-6000 3D scanning instrument, Zeiss scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as well as Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDS) analyses. Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS was used to ascertain the surfaces states.

Quantum chemical calculations were conducted using Gaussian 09W software. The CDs molecule was optimized based on the density functional theory (DFT), using the B3LYP method with 6–311++G (d, p) set. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and the energy gap (ΔE = E HOMO‒ E LUMO) were analyzed to investigate the correlation between molecular characteristics and corrosion inhibition behavior. Moreover, molecular dynamic (MD) simulations were carried out using the Forcite module to calculate the adsorption energy (E binding) between CDs and the carbon steel surface. Fe (110) substrate and γ-FeOOH (010) substrate were considered as potential substrates based on their stable crystal structures [42,43]. The Fe (110) interface model consisted of 6 layers with a total of 3240 Fe atoms and a dimension of 37.26 Å × 28.38 Å × 38.11 Å. The γ-FeOOH (010) interface model was comprised of 2 layers with a dimension of 36.48 Å × 28.96 Å × 43.54 Å, including a total of 396 Fe atoms, 792 O atoms, and 396 H atoms. Finally, MD simulations were conducted in an NVT system at 298 K for 500 ps using the COMPASSIII force field with a time interval of 1 fs.

As shown in Fig. 1 , a facile low-temperature method was employed to prepare CDs (shown in Chapter 2.2). The Schiff base reaction occurred between the CO groups of p-BQ and the –NH2 groups of o-PD. Polymer fragments were then formed via dehydration, condensation, and polymerization. Carbonization and aromatization of fragments generated aromatic clusters, which eventually transformed into CDs. This low-cost, and facile approach avoided the time-consuming and energy-intensive preparation protocols.

TEM was utilized to characterize the particle size distribution and morphology of CDs. TEM analysis clearly showed that the CDs were monodisperse and quasi-spherical, without aggregation. The size distribution of CDs ranged from 2.52 to 10.50 nm with an average size of 6.30 nm by measuring more than 100 particles (Fig. 2 a). Moreover, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images revealed that most CDs possessed crystalline structures, as evidenced by clear observation of lattice spacing of 0.24 nm which corresponded to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphite (Fig. 2b) [44]. The AFM images (Fig. 2c and d) showed that the topographic heights of CDs were about 2.0 nm; And verified that the CDs were well dispersed, in good agreement with the TEM results. The characterization from TEM and AFM testified that CDs with good dispersity were successfully prepared by the intentionally designed method.

Fig. 3 a displayed the FTIR curves of o-PD, p-BQ, and CDs. The adsorption peaks at 1498 cm−1 (C–N) and 3360-3380 cm−1 (N–H) suggested the presence of an amine group (‒NH2) from o-PD. The strong characteristic peak at 1643 cm−1 could be attributed to the carbonyl group (CO) of p-BQ [45]. Furthermore, the CDs spectra showed the adsorption peak at 1630 cm−1 corresponding to CN, which implied that the synthesis of CDs was a result of the Schiff base reaction between the N–H group of o-PD and the CO group of p-BQ. Moreover, the CDs peaks between 3200 and 3400 cm−1 were attributed to O–H/N–H bonds, and the peak at 1490 cm−1 was from C–N [46]. XPS measurement corroborated the findings derived from FTIR spectra. The full-range XPS spectra included three peaks at 285, 532, and 399 eV, ascribed to C1s, O1s, and N1s respectively. And the relative atomic contents were 76.93 % for C, 12.77 % for O, and 10.31 % for N, respectively. In the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 3b), four peaks were assigned to CC/C–C (284.2 eV), C–N (285.4 eV), C–OH (286.3 eV), and CN/CO (288.3 eV). The O1s spectra (Fig. 3c) involved two fitting peaks: CO (530.2 eV) and C–O (532.3 eV). The N1s spectra (Fig. 3d) revealed the presence of CN (398.6 eV), N–H (399.8 eV), and –NO2 (406.1 eV). Notably, the CN bond took the highest amount, further demonstrating the presence of Schiff base structures in CDs. In conclusion, FTIR and XPS investigations convincingly illustrated that O- and N-contained structures were abundant in CDs, which endow them with a high corrosion inhibition ability for carbon steel in SCPS.

Fig. 4 presented the optical properties of CDs. In Fig. 4a, CDs demonstrated two adsorption peaks at 285 and 375 nm, attributed to π‒π* and n‒π* transitions from the CC bond and O- and N-based groups, respectively [47]. The inset image of the CDs’ aqueous solution with the concentration of 1 mg/mL exhibited a faint yellow color under natural light, and emitted a bright yellow photoluminescence (PL) under 365 nm UV light. Furthermore, the CDs illustrated excitation-independent fluorescence with maximum excitation/emission at 430/576 nm (Fig. 4b). The aforementioned optical characteristics provided a new approach to evaluate the dispersion stability of CDs in SCPS.

In summary, the above characterization indicated that CDs with an average diameter of 6.30 nm were successfully prepared through a time-saving and energy-efficient approach, based on the Schiff base reaction between o-PD and p-BQ. More importantly, the resultant CDs demonstrated an eminent long-term dispersion stability and possessed numerous O- and N-containing functional groups. These features conferred potential exceptional corrosion inhibition performance on CDs, conducing to their use as a novel corrosion inhibitor in cement-based materials.

As commonly acknowledged, corrosion inhibitors require high dispersion stability for their practical application [48]. Prior to electrochemical measurements, it was necessary to determine the dispersion stability of as-prepared CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, after 24 h of standing at room temperature, no sedimentation was observed in the SCPS solutions containing CDs at different concentrations. When exposed to daylight, the blank SCPS solution remained colorless; While the addition of CDs endowed the solution with faint yellow color, and the color of the solutions gradually changed to orange-yellow with the increase of CDs. Under UV light excitation, no PL was observed from the blank SCPS solution by the naked eye. In sharp contrast, the incorporation of CDs resulted in blue PL emission, and the intensity of PL emission gradually enhanced with the augment of CDs concentration. It should be pointed out that the aggregation of CDs leads to the PL quenching effect [49]. Thus, the finding above qualitatively confirmed that CDs dispersed well without observable aggregation, possessing excellent dispersion stability in SCPS solution. To quantitatively analyze the long-term dispersion stability of CDs, UV–vis and PL spectra were carried out (Fig. 5). The intensities of UV–vis adsorption peaks at approximately 260 and 425 nm from the CDs-containing SCPS were directly proportional to the concentration of CDs, as revealed by the curves in Fig. 5a. And the intensities of their maximum PL emission peaks at around 537 nm exhibited an identical trend with that of the UV–vis adsorption peaks (Fig. 5b). It is worth mentioning that the PL emission peak of CDs in SCPS underwent a blue shift, ascribed to the deprotonation of CDs. Crucially, the peak intensities of all CDs-incorporating solutions remained almost unchanged, as evidenced by the UV–vis (Fig. 5a) and maximum PL emission spectra (Fig. 5b). The observations quantitatively confirmed the great dispersion stability of CDs in SCPS, consistent with qualitative findings. Hence, both qualitative and quantitative analyses disclosed the distinguished long-term dispersion stability of CDs in SCPS, establishing a strong basis for their utilization as a novel corrosion inhibitor in concrete with an alkaline environment.

To investigate the inhibition properties of CDs for carbon steel in Cl-SCPS, EIS and PDP measurements were conducted. EIS results of carbon steel following 2-day immersion were depicted in Fig. 6 a. Compared with the C0 sample, C100, C200, C300, and C400 samples owned larger capacitive loops, indicating that CDs effectively suppressed steel corrosion in Cl-SCPS [50]. As the concentration of CDs increased, the diameter of capacitive loops enlarged, suggesting an enhanced inhibition ability. After 20-day immersion, the capacitive loops of all the samples showed the same trend, implying notable long-term protection of CDs. Furthermore, compared with those of C100, C200, C300, and C400 samples immersed in Cl-SCPS for 2 d, their capacitive loops after 20-day immersion did not exhibit significant reductions; In sharp contrast, the capacitive loop of the C0 sample exposed to Cl-SCPS for 20 d went through a remarkable decrease (Fig. 6c). This finding demonstrated that the severe chloride attack was alleviated by CDs. As is well known, the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency (0.01 Hz) in Bode plots, namely |Z|0.01 Hz, serves as an indicator of the corrosion inhibition ability of the corrosion inhibitor [51,52]. As revealed in Fig. 6b, the |Z|0.01 Hz values of C0, C100, C200, C300, and C400 groups were 2.2, 2.6, 10.1, 18.6, and 66.7 kΩ cm2, respectively. The higher the CDs concentration, the larger the |Z|0.01 Hz value, implying a positive correlation between the corrosion inhibition performance and CDs content. A similar tendency was observed with an extended immersion time of 20 d (Fig. 6d). In detail, the |Z|0.01 Hz values of C100, C200, C300, and C400 samples boost by 91.1 %, 95.5 %, 97.3 %, and 98.8 % respectively, compared with that of C0 group, further indicating the long-term protection of CDs against chloride corrosion. In addition, all the maximum phase angles in Bode plots (Fig. 6b and d) moved higher with the augment of CDs concentration regardless of the immersion for 2 or 20 d, demonstrating CDs adsorption at the steel/solution interface [50,53].

To interpret EIS results, an equivalent circuit model with one time constant (Fig. 6c) was employed, since there was only one capacitive loop in all the Nyquist plots [54]. And this model comprised solution resistance (R
s ), polarization resistance (R
p ), and double-layer capacitance (CPEdl). Corresponding parameters were tabulated in Table 1 , and all the data fitted well with a low chi-squared value of 10−3. With the incorporation of CDs, R
s values were virtually unchanged irrespective of immersion time, indicating that CDs had an insignificant influence on R
s . After 2-day immersion in Cl-SCPS, R
p gradually rose from 2138 to 52240 Ω cm2 with the addition of CDs, suggesting that the polarization process was effectively hindered by CDs adsorption [51]. With extending immersion time to 20 d, the R
p value of the C0 sample extensively decreased an order of magnitude (from 2138 to 486 Ω cm2), indicating a severe loss of corrosion resistance. In stark contrast, the values of C100, C200, C300, and C400 demonstrated a limited decrease, indicating that CDs adsorption on the mental surface effectively enhanced the corrosion resistance of samples [55]. Furthermore, CPEdl, related to the corrosion reaction area, decreased with the increasing of CDs concentration, inferring that the reduction of corrosion area caused by CDs resulted in effective protection for carbon steel [37,56]. The n value was the exponent of CPEdl (−1< n <1), and CPEdl = Y 0(2πf max) n−1. The n value increased close to 1 with the addition of CDs, signifying that higher CDs concentration could lead to a smoother corrosion interface [57]. The inhibition efficiency (η) was calculated by the equation: η = (R
p - R
p
0 )/R
p
×100 %, where R
p and R
p
0 denoted the charge transfer resistances with and without CDs, respectively [58]. The η values increased with heightening CDs concentration, and the C400 sample held the highest η value of 95.9 % in all the samples immersed for 2 d. Notably, following a 20-day immersion, all the η values significantly increased, and the η value of the C400 sample reached the highest 99.0 %, substantiating the exceptional long-term corrosion inhibition performance of CDs for carbon steel against chloride attack.

The inhibiting effect of CDs was further assessed using the PDP technique. Fig. 7 compared the PDP curves of C0, C100, C200, C300, and C400 samples. Classical extrapolation was performed to determine the Tafel segment of the PDP curve, yielding correlation parameters presented in Table 2 such as the corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), cathode slope (β c), anode slope (β a), and inhibition efficiency (IE). The addition of CDs gave rise to a noticeable decrease in both β c and β a, accompanied with a significant reduction in i corr values (at least one order of magnitude lower than that of the C0 sample). These observations strongly evidenced that CDs functioned as a mixed-type inhibitor, efficiently inhibiting both cathodic and anodic reactions. More specifically, the β a values decreased shaper compared to the β c values, indicating that the CDs primarily adsorbed onto active anodic sites, thus enormously impeding the anodic dissolution rate of iron from the carbon steel [36]. The E corr value shifted toward more positive values with the addition of CDs, indicating the adsorption of CDs on the steel surface. Furthermore, the IE values were calculated by the following formula: IE= (i corr(0) - i corr(inh))/i corr(0) × 100 %, where i corr(0) and i corr(inh) represented the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of CDs, respectively [59]. With the incorporation of CDs, all the IE values exceeded 89.5 %, with the C400 sample exhibiting an impressive IE value up to 99.2 %. This finding was greatly consistent with the EIS result, further substantiating the long-term corrosion protection effect of CDs for carbon steel against chloride attack.

To sum up, EIS and PDP measurements corroborated that CDs, synthesized by a facile low-temperature method, effectively suppressed the corrosion of carbon steel Cl-SCPS. More importantly, CDs possessed long-term corrosion inhibition capability with a remarkable inhibition efficiency of 99.2 %. This was possibly attributed to the adsorption of CDs onto carbon steel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition mechanism of CDs-based corrosion inhibitors

ce:section.ce:para:
For a better understanding of the interaction mechanism between the CDs-based corrosion inhibitors and mental surface, isothermal adsorption curves were fitted based on EIS data and PDP data (Fig. 8 ). The relationship between C inh/θ and C inh was calculated by the Equation [60]:

Where θ was the surface coverage; C inh and K ads represented the inhibitor concentration and the adsorption equilibrium constant respectively. The results indicated that the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model was suitable for the adsorption process of CDs since a strong linear correlation was displayed with R2 values of 0.9997 and 0.9998 for the fitted line from EIS (Fig. 8a) and PDP (Fig. 8b), respectively. The adsorption-free energy (

Herein, R was the molar gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), and T denoted the absolute temperature (298 K). The calculated ΔG 0 ads values from PDP and EIS measurements were −23.98 and −24.09 kJ/mol respectively, suggesting that the CDs-based corrosion inhibitors have a great adsorption tendency on the steel surface.

To further investigate the inhibition mechanism of the CDs-based corrosion inhibitor, the surfaces of all the specimens after EIS tests were characterized using SEM, EDS, 3D morphology instrument, and XPS. As demonstrated in Fig. 9 a, the original carbon steel surface was uniform and smooth with only minor polishing-induced scratches. By EDS elemental mapping results, primary elements containing Fe, O, and C illustrated uniform distribution on the surface of carbon steel. After 20-day immersion in Cl-SCPS without CDs, massive bulges induced by corrosion productions emerged, accompanied with diminished Fe element and newly emerging Cl element (Fig. 9b). This observation confirmed severe corrosion occurred under chloride adsorption [61], thus producing Cl-containing corrosion products such as Fe(OH)Cl2, Fe(OH)2Cl, and FeCl3. Conversely, the surface of carbon steel protected by 400 mg/L CDs was considerably smoother and more homogeneous without notable corrosion products (Fig. 9c), inferring that the chloride-caused corrosion was effectively prohibited by the CDs-based corrosion inhibitors. Moreover, the Fe element on the surface of carbon steel shared a similar distribution with that of original carbon steel, since CDs hindered Fe dissolution. More specifically, as revealed in Table 3 , all the sample surfaces predominantly contained Fe, O, and C elements, with a significant quantity of Fe. Regarding the original carbon steel, it possessed a Fe element content of 80.11 % and an O element content of 3.26 %. Upon immersion in Cl-SCPS without CDs for 20 d, the Fe element content on the steel surface remarkably decreased to 38.98 %, and the O element content synchronously increased to 18.34 %, further suggesting that O-containing corrosion products (involving α-FeOOH, γ-FeOOH, β-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3, and Fe3O4) caused by chloride ions were formed on the metal surface [62]. Meanwhile, the Cl element accounted for the largest proportion (5.47 %) among the three samples, resulting from chloride adsorption. For carbon steel immersed in CDs-including Cl-SCPS, the Fe element content (75.76 %) presented slightly lower than that of original carbon steel, while the Cl atom content dropped to 0.13 %, testifying that CDs efficiently inhibited the chloride-caused corrosion.

3D corrosion morphology analyses provided more detailed information from the perspective of surface roughness. As illustrated in Fig. 10 a, the original steel sample showed a flat surface with a surface roughness parameter (S a) of 0.801 μm; Evidently, after 20-day immersion in Cl-SCPS (Fig. 10b), the steel specimen was severely corroded by chloride ions with the formation of multiple corrosion pits. And the depth of the largest pit reached approximately 150 μm, leading to a greater S a value of 4.992 μm. While with the introduction of CDs, the S a value returned to a lower value of 1.551 μm (Fig. 10c), reducing by 68.9 % to that of carbon steel without the CDs protection. It should be noted that the corrosion products on the steel surface were treated by acid pickling to ascertain the depth of the large corrosion pit. The 3D morphology analyses further corroborated the findings of SEM/EDS, confirming that the CDs-based corrosion inhibitors effectively inhibited the formation of pitting corrosion arising from chloride ions.

In conclusion, the comprehensive corrosion morphology analyses evidenced that the CDs-based corrosion inhibitors could effectively safeguard carbon steel against chloride-induced corrosion, potentially attributed to the CDs adsorption onto the steel surface. To further testify the inhibition mechanism of CDs, theoretical calculation methods were expected to be employed.

Considering the deployment of CDs inhibitors in the liquid phase, the optimal adsorption configuration of CDs inhibitors was simulated in the liquid environment. Firstly, based on DFT, the structure-reactivity correlation of CDs molecules was analyzed by the quantum chemical investigation. According to the FTIR and XPS results (Fig. 3), CDs exhibited a limited number of CO bonds from carboxyl groups and revealed the existence of hydroxyl, amino, and CN groups. In light of these observations, a simplified structure of the as-synthesized CDs was established (Fig. 12 ). And the frontier molecular orbital of CDs and the precursors were presented in Fig. 13 . It could be observed that the HOMO and LUMO spread on the entire molecule of the CDs-based corrosion inhibitors, indicating their flat adsorption on the steel surface. In general, a low E LUMO value indicates a strong electron-accepting ability, while a high E HOMO value molecule is inclined to donate electrons [69]. Hence, the difference value between E HOMO and E LUMO, namely the energy gap (ΔE), is a symbol of the charge donation ability of inhibitor molecules to metal surfaces during the corrosion mitigation process. In other words, the smaller the value of ΔE, the greater the inhibition effect of inhibitors on the metal surface [70]. The quantitative parameters were listed in Table 5 . When compared to the precursors of o-PD and p-BQ, CDs exhibited a lower ΔE, indicating higher chemical reactivity and greater inhibition capacity.

The surface of steel embedding in RC structures falls into two states: the passivation state dominated by γ-FeOOH, and the defected-induced Fe matrix exposed state [43]. Hence, it is necessary to investigate both the Fe and γ-FeOOH substrates. Figs. 14 and 15
presented the equilibrium adsorption configuration of three kinds of molecules (CDs and the two precursors) on the Fe (110) and γ-FeOOH (010) substrates, respectively. Evidently, all the molecules were parallelly adsorbed onto the surface of two substrates, meaning that the three types of molecules could completely spread on the metal surface. Table 6 displayed their adsorption energies on the Fe (110) and γ-FeOOH (010) surfaces. The calculated E binding values of o-PD, p-BQ, and CDs on the Fe (110) surface were −92.38, −85.35, and −208.44 kJ mol−1, respectively. Generally speaking, the more negative the E binding value is, the more stable the adsorption behavior is [70]. Thus, CDs with the highest E binding absolute value possessed the stronger interaction strength to the Fe (110) surface. Identically, CDs also owned a higher E binding absolute value (−83.48 kJ mol−1) than o-PD (−37.30 kJ mol−1) and p-BQ (−32.51 kJ mol−1) on the γ-FeOOH (010) surface, inferring the stronger adsorption ability of CDs. As a result, CDs undoubtedly demonstrated an improved inhibiting capacity irrespective of Fe or γ-FeOOH(010) surfaces. It should be pointed out that compared with the previous literature, in which the calculated E binding values of the CDs on the Fe (110) surface was less than 191 kJ mol−1, the CDs-based corrosion inhibitors in this work held a distinctly higher E binding absolute value. This further disclosed that the as-prepared CDs could play a better role as an efficient corrosion inhibitor. To sum up, the as-synthesized CDs served as an effective corrosion inhibitor adsorbed on the carbon steel in Cl-SCPS against aggressive chloride ions.

Based on the adsorption isotherm analyses, corrosion morphology, and theoretical calculation, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDs was reasonably proposed. Namely, CDs could stably adsorb on the steel surface. As shown in Fig. 16 a, the aggressive chloride ions in Cl-SCPS adsorbed on carbon steel surfaces, accelerating the dissolution of Fe2+ ions. On account of their self-catalytic effect, chloride ions failed to be consumed, thus continuously promoting the dissolution of the passive film [71]. In this situation, the reaction equation was as follows [54,61]:
Anodic
reaction : Fe + 2
Cl ‒
→
Fe
2 +
+ 2
e −
+ 2
Cl ‒
Cathodic
reaction : 2
H 2
O +
O 2
+ 2
e ‒
→ 2
OH ‒

As illustrated in Fig. 16b, owing to their numerous O- and N-containing functional groups (proved by FTIR and XPS) and high dispersion stability in the alkaline SCPS (demonstrated by UV–vis and PL spectra), CDs could uniformly adsorb onto the carbon steel surface. The former occurred by the formation of coordination bonds between lone pair electrons of CDs' O/N atom and the Fe atoms’ vacant 3d orbital of carbon steel; While the latter was attributed to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and steel surface. Sequentially, CDs molecules hindered aggressive chloride ions from adsorbing onto the steel surface, thereby remarkably reducing the chloride-incorporated corrosion products (testified by SEM and EDS) and the chloride-induced corrosion pits (evidenced by 3D morphology analysis). Consequently, the passive film of carbon steel under the protection of CDs exhibited a higher Fe2+/Fe3+ ratio (confirmed by XPS), providing the steel matrix with stronger protection under chloride corrosion. And as revealed by EIS and PDP tests, with the increasing of CDs concentration, the corrosion potential shifted towards the more positive value, indicating that CDs mainly hindered the anodic dissolution, thereby decreasing the polarization current density. Significantly, the long-term corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs achieved 99 % only at 400 mg/L.

The DFT and MD simulations further confirmed the effective corrosion inhibition ability of the CDs-based corrosion inhibitor. Among three molecules (CDs and two kinds of precursors), CDs with the smallest ΔE value possessed the largest reactivity, and the highest preference for transferring electrons to carbon steel. Meanwhile, CDs owned a higher E binding absolute value on Fe (110) substrate than other CDs reported in previous literature, substantiating that the as-prepared CDs could achieve better adsorption onto Fe (110) substrate, thus providing more outstanding protection against chloride ions. Furthermore, the E binding absolute value of CDs on the γ-FeOOH (010) surface was smaller than that of CDs on the Fe (110) substrate. Hence, CDs tended to adsorb onto Fe (110) substrate, conducing to better protect the carbon steel after the chloride-induced damage of the passive film.

In this study, CDs were successfully prepared by a facile low-temperature method, and first served as a novel green corrosion inhibitor to efficiently protect carbon steel against chloride corrosion in Cl-SCPS. More importantly, their inhibition mechanism was deeply investigated. The main conclusions were summarized as follows.
(1)
Based on the Schiff base reaction, the CDs with abundant O- and N-containing groups were simply prepared. Crucially, the CDs possessed excellent dispersion stability in SCPS determined by qualitative and quantitative analyses, laying a strong basis for their utilization as corrosion inhibitors.
(2)
According to EIS and PDP measurements, CDs substantiated an exceptional long-term corrosion inhibition behavior with the highest inhibition efficiency of 99.2 % at only 400 mg/L. Moreover, CDs acted as a mixed-type corrosion inhibitor, but dominantly hindered the anodic reaction.
(3)
In light of adsorption isotherm analysis, corrosion morphology investigation, and theoretical calculation, the inhibition mechanism of CDs could be assigned to the stable adsorption on the steel surface.

All these findings above demonstrated that CDs could provide an excellent corrosion protection effect for carbon steel in Cl-SCPS, expected to be employed to resist chloride-induced corrosion for RC structures.
